name: Number Series

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - 8 real past-paper number series questions, independently re-verified
2017_q1 page 61 Q169 (21st term of an AP, with answer key, image-verified)
2024_q page 29 Q40 (n(n+1)² missing-term pattern, with answer key, image-verified)
2025_q1 page 83 Q146 (×0.5/×1.5/×2.5... escalating-multiplier pattern, with answer key, image-verified)
2026_q Q84-86 odd-one-out set, pulled from aptitude-evidence-stash.md where it had been sitting unused since the original research pass

researchNotes: This is the LAST of Part B's 6 named Reasoning skills, completing all of Part B (Aptitude & Mental Ability) entirely. 8 sourced questions, every single one independently re-derived and verified by hand before use (one item's source-site number formatting was garbled — likely 'prime² ' exponents flattened into plain digits — caught and corrected to present the actual mathematical pattern rather than propagate the confusing raw text). Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned the OCR bank with 'find the next/missing term' keywords and found 3 more genuine, image-verified past-paper hits (an AP nth-term question landing on the trap answer 0, an n(n+1)² disguised-square pattern, and an escalating-multiplier ×0.5/×1.5/×2.5/... pattern) plus drained a 3-question odd-one-out set (×11 pairs, perfect-cube spotting, word-pair classification) that had been sitting unused in the aptitude evidence stash since the original build. Added a solving-shortcut hint to every single question in this topic (including the pre-existing ones, which had none) since the instructions mandate it for every Number Series item and this file predates that requirement — additive-only change, no question/answer content was altered. Bug fix (Batch 20): the reading qa[] hints had been added under the wrong field name ('trick', which only exists on mockTest.questions[]) — the zod schema silently stripped them since QASchema only recognises 'tip'. Renamed all 13 to 'tip' so they actually reach the UI; mockTest.questions[] entries already correctly used 'trick' and were untouched.

reading.intro: Number Series is the final Reasoning skill. Real exam evidence shows several distinct underlying patterns: second-difference (difference-of-differences) sequences, sum-of-consecutive-cubes, geometric progression, arithmetic progression (including ones that land on a trap answer like 0), n² multiples, n(n+1)² disguised patterns, escalating-multiplier sequences, and odd-one-out spotting (numeric or word-pair) — recognize the pattern TYPE before trying to compute term-by-term.

qa.question: Method — sum of consecutive cubes: Find the next term: 9, 35, 91, ?
qa.tip: Test small-n cube sums FIRST whenever consecutive terms grow much faster than a simple AP/GP — cube-sum series jump by large, uneven amounts that don't fit a constant ratio or difference.

qa.question: Method — second-difference (arithmetic sequence of differences): Fill in the blank: 1, 6, 15, 28, 45, ___, 91.
qa.answer: Differences: 6−1=5, 15−6=9, 28−15=13, 45−28=17 — these differences themselves increase by 4 each time. Next difference = 17+4=21, so the blank = 45+21 = 66. (Check: 91−66=25=21+4 ✓ consistent.)
qa.tip: Whenever the first round of differences isn't constant, take differences of THOSE differences (2nd-order) — if that row IS constant, you have a quadratic-style series; extend outward from there.

qa.question: Method — sequence of squared primes: Find the missing number in the sequence of squared prime numbers: 4 (2²), 9 (3²), 25 (5²), 49 (7²), ?, 169 (13²).
qa.answer: The base numbers are consecutive primes: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13. The missing term corresponds to 11² = 121. (Caution: the original source rendered this sequence with garbled formatting that looked like plain 3-digit numbers rather than squared primes — always re-derive the actual pattern rather than trusting a confusing transcription.)
qa.tip: If every visible term is a perfect square, check whether the square ROOTS form a recognizable sequence (like consecutive primes) rather than treating the big numbers themselves as the pattern.

qa.question: Method — simple arithmetic progression (AP) with increasing-by-2 differences: Find the next term: 1, 5, 11, 19, 29, ?
qa.answer: Differences: 4, 6, 8, 10 — increasing by 2 each time. Next difference = 12, so next term = 29+12 = 41.
qa.tip: Write out the difference row first, every time, before guessing — an evenly-increasing difference row (here +2 each step) is the single most common TNPSC series shape.

qa.question: Method — geometric progression (GP): Find the 8th term of 2, 6, 18, 54, ...
qa.answer: Common ratio = 3 (each term ×3). Terms: 2,6,18,54,162,486,1458,4374. The 8th term is 4,374.
qa.tip: Confirm the ratio by dividing at least 2 consecutive pairs (6/2=3, 18/6=3) before committing — a single division can coincidentally match a wrong ratio.

qa.question: Method — plain arithmetic progression, nth term formula: Find the 20th term of 7, 10, 13, ...
qa.answer: First term=7, common difference=3. nth term = a+(n−1)d = 7+(19×3) = 7+57 = 64.
qa.tip: For any 'find the Nth term' AP question, always use a+(n−1)d directly instead of writing out all N terms — far fewer chances for arithmetic slips.

qa.question: Method — multiples of n²: Find the missing number: 3, 12, 27, 48, 75, 108, ___.
qa.tip: Divide each term by its position number squared (n²) — if the quotient is the SAME constant every time, you've found an n² multiple series instantly.

qa.question: Real exam (2017_q1 page 61 Q169): What is the 21st term of the sequence 100, 95, 90, 85, ...?
qa.answer: This is an AP with a=100, d=−5. 21st term = a+(n−1)d = 100+(20×−5) = 100−100 = 0 — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: A negative common difference can legitimately drive the sequence down to exactly 0 (or negative) — don't assume 0 must be a distractor just because it 'looks like a trick answer'; verify with the formula.

qa.question: Real exam (2025_q1 page 83 Q146): Find the next term: 64, 32, 48, 120, 420, ?
qa.answer: The multiplier between consecutive terms itself increases by 1 each step: ×0.5 (64→32), ×1.5 (32→48), ×2.5 (48→120), ×3.5 (120→420), so the next multiplier is ×4.5: 420×4.5 = 1,890 — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: If ratios between consecutive terms look irregular at first glance, check whether the RATIO itself forms a simple pattern (here, increasing by exactly 1 each step: 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 4.5) rather than giving up and assuming there's no multiplicative pattern.

qa.question: Real exam (2024_q page 29 Q40): Find the missing number in the series: 4, 18, ?, 100, 180, 294, 448.
qa.answer: Divide each term by its position n: 4/1=4, 18/2=9, ?/3=?, 100/4=25, 180/5=36, 294/6=49, 448/7=64. Those quotients are perfect squares of (n+1): 2²,3²,4²,5²,6²,7²,8² — so term/n = (n+1)², meaning term = n(n+1)². For n=3: 3×4²=3×16=48 — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: When a series grows very fast and irregularly, try dividing each term by its position number (n) first — the quotient often reveals a much simpler hidden pattern (here, perfect squares) that's invisible in the raw numbers.

qa.question: Real exam (2026_q Q84, from the stash): Choose the number pair that doesn't match the pattern of the others: 17,187 / 14,134 / 12,132 / 15,165.
qa.tip: For 'odd pair' questions, test the same single operation (here ×11) on EVERY pair rather than eyeballing — the odd one usually fails a very simple, consistent rule.

qa.question: Real exam (2026_q Q85, from the stash): Choose the number that doesn't belong: 225, 125, 64, 216.
qa.answer: 125=5³, 64=4³, 216=6³ are all perfect cubes — but 225=15² is a perfect SQUARE, not a cube. 225 is the odd one out.
qa.tip: When 3 of 4 numbers are suspiciously 'round' (perfect cubes/squares), quickly test cube roots and square roots on all four — the odd one will fail the cube test but often pass a different one (square, here).

qa.question: Real exam (2026_q Q86, from the stash): Choose the odd pair: Odometer:Current, Barometer:Pressure, Seismograph:Earthquakes, Thermometer:Temperature.
qa.answer: Odometer:Current is the odd pair — an odometer measures distance/speed travelled, NOT current (an ammeter measures current). The other three pairs correctly match instrument to what it measures.
qa.tip: This is a classification/analogy item that appeared on the same exam page as pure-number series questions — TNPSC's 'Number Series' section sometimes bundles in odd-one-out word-pair questions too, so don't assume every question on that page must be purely numeric.

questions.trick: Subtract 1 from each term and check if what's left is a perfect cube (1,8,27,64,125,...) — confirms the n³+1 pattern fast.

questions.explanation: Differences: 5,7,9,11 — increasing by 2. Next difference=13, so blank=34+13=47. (Check: 62−47=15=13+2, consistent.)
questions.trick: Always sanity-check a filled-in blank against the NEXT given term too, not just the one before it — here 62−47=15 correctly continues the +2 difference pattern.

questions.explanation: Differences: 4,8,12,16 — increasing by 4. Next difference=20, so next term=43+20=63.
questions.trick: A difference row that itself increases by a constant amount (here +4) means you can always extend it by simple addition, no formula needed.

questions.prompt: Find the 7th term of the GP: 3, 9, 27, 81, ...
questions.trick: Write out each multiplication step (×3 each time) rather than trying to compute 3⁷ mentally — fewer slips under time pressure.

questions.prompt: Find the 15th term of the AP: 5, 9, 13, 17, ...
questions.trick: nth term = a+(n−1)d — plug in directly instead of listing all 15 terms.
Divide each term by its position squared (n²) — a constant quotient (here, 2) confirms an n² multiple series instantly.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2017_q1 Q169): What is the 21st term of the sequence 100, 95, 90, 85, ...?
questions.trick: Don't assume '0' is a fake distractor in an AP question — a negative common difference can legitimately reach exactly zero.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2025_q1 Q146): Find the next term: 64, 32, 48, 120, 420, ?
questions.explanation: The multiplier increases by 1 each step: ×0.5, ×1.5, ×2.5, ×3.5, then ×4.5. 420×4.5=1,890.
questions.trick: When ratios between terms look irregular, check whether the ratio ITSELF increases in a simple pattern (here, by exactly 1 each time: 0.5→1.5→2.5→3.5→4.5).

questions.prompt: Real exam (2024_q Q40): Find the missing number: 4, 18, ?, 100, 180, 294, 448.
questions.explanation: term = n(n+1)²: n=1→1×4=4, n=2→2×9=18, n=3→3×16=48, n=4→4×25=100, matching the rest of the series.
questions.trick: Divide each term by its position (n) — the quotient often reveals a simple perfect-square pattern hiding inside a fast-growing series.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2026_q Q84): Which number pair breaks the ×11 pattern seen in the others: 17,187 / 14,134 / 12,132 / 15,165?
questions.trick: Apply the same single operation (×11) to every pair — the odd one will fail it cleanly.

questions.prompt: Real exam (2026_q Q85): Which of these numbers is NOT a perfect cube: 225, 125, 64, 216?
questions.explanation: 125=5³, 64=4³, 216=6³ are perfect cubes; 225=15² is a perfect square, not a cube.
questions.trick: Memorize cubes 1³ through 10³ (1,8,27,64,125,216,343,512,729,1000) — spotting a non-cube among them becomes instant recall, not calculation.

questions.prompt: Find the next term (Fibonacci-style, each term = sum of the previous two): 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ?
questions.trick: If each term looks close to the sum of the two before it, test that rule directly (a Fibonacci-style series) — it's one of the fastest patterns to confirm.

questions.prompt: Find the next term (each term = previous term ×2, plus 1): 3, 7, 15, 31, 63, ?
questions.trick: When each term is roughly double the last, test 'previous×2 plus/minus a small constant' — a very common TNPSC series shape.

questions.prompt: Find the 25th term of the AP: 80, 75, 70, 65, ...
questions.trick: A descending AP with enough terms can legitimately go negative — apply the formula and trust the arithmetic rather than assuming a 'clean' positive answer.

questions.prompt: Find the missing number in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, 42, ___.
questions.trick: Terms of the form n(n+1) grow as consecutive-integer products — check if adjacent small integers multiply to each term before assuming a harder pattern.

questions.prompt: Which number does NOT belong with the others: 27, 64, 125, 144?
questions.explanation: 27=3³, 64=4³, 125=5³ are perfect cubes; 144=12² is a perfect square, not a cube.
questions.trick: Same cube-list-memorization trick as the real 225/125/64/216 exam question — instantly spot the non-cube.

questions.prompt: Find the 6th term of the GP: 5, 15, 45, ...
questions.trick: Multiply step by step by the common ratio (×3) rather than computing 5×3⁵ mentally.

questions.prompt: Choose the odd pair: Odometer:Distance, Barometer:Pressure, Ammeter:Weight, Thermometer:Temperature.
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures electric current, not weight — the other three pairs correctly match instrument to what it measures.
questions.trick: For instrument:measurement pairs, mentally confirm each instrument's actual job before trusting the stated pairing.
